retrieved_docs.title: Croup -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Approximately 5% of children treated for croup in the outpatient setting have repeat visits for recurrent symptoms within 7 days. Children with recurrent croup episodes should be referred to an otolaryngologist to evaluate for underlying airway abnormalities, such as laryngomalacia, subglottic stenosis, gastroesophageal reflux disease pathology, or hemangiomas. Airway abnormalities are identified in approximately 10% of children with recurrent croup referred to otolaryngologists and 45% of those who undergo airway endoscopy. [21] [22] [23]

retrieved_docs.title: Croup -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Croup is a common respiratory illness that affects the trachea, larynx, and bronchi, leading to inspiratory stridor and barking cough. This infection is a self-limited disease typically observed in children younger than 5. The clinical spectrum includes laryngotracheitis, laryngotracheobronchitis, and laryngotracheobronchopneumonitis. Most children with croup improve with a single dose of dexamethasone and supportive management. However, a few with severe symptoms require racemic epinephrine in addition to dexamethasone. Uncommon complications include bacterial tracheitis, pneumonia, pulmonary edema, and, rarely, death. This activity reviews the evaluation and management of croup, emphasizing the importance of the interprofessional team in improving care for patients with this condition. As an upper respiratory condition, stridor is the presenting symptom, which must be differentiated from wheezing caused by lower respiratory conditions. This activity emphasizes the importance of distinguishing between upper and lower respiratory signs and symptoms, as this differentiation guides appropriate management. The activity also discusses the differential diagnosis of croup and its management, highlighting the need for prompt differentiation of conditions, especially severe and rapidly deteriorating conditions such as epiglottitis, which require intensive care admission and actions to secure the airway.

retrieved_docs.title: Croup -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with mild croup have an excellent prognosis. Hospital admission and mortality rates are low and vary significantly between communities. Fewer than 0.5% of intubated children died in a 10-year study of hospitalized children with croup. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Croup -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Cough Medicine: Guidelines discourage the use of cough medicines, which typically contain dextromethorphan or guaifenesin. These medications can cause drowsiness in a child, requiring increased effort to breathe, which may exacerbate the symptoms of croup.[ U.S. Food and Drug Administration. Use Caution When Giving Cough and Cold Products to Kids ]

retrieved_docs.title: Croup -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Symptoms of mild croup subside in the majority of children within 2 days. However, a few patients have symptoms that persist for up to 1 week. Moderate-to-severe croup symptoms need treatment with epinephrine in addition to glucocorticoids. Complications such as pneumonia, pulmonary edema, and secondary bacterial infection may occur, leading to tracheitis, requiring antibiotics. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Croup -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Based on the Westley croup score, treatment depends on the severity of signs and symptoms. Children with mild croup receive a single dose of dexamethasone, symptomatic treatment, and supportive care. Children with moderate-to-severe croup are given nebulized epinephrine and dexamethasone. [14] All patients with diminished oxygen saturation should receive supplemental oxygen. Moderate-to-severe cases require observation and admission if the symptoms do not improve. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Croup -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Although croup is primarily a clinical diagnosis, laboratory studies and imaging can guide management in selected cases. Nasopharyngeal swabs for influenza, respiratory syncytial virus, and parainfluenza serologies may confirm alternative diagnoses and change management. Clinicians must differentiate other obstructive conditions, such as epiglottitis, bacterial tracheitis, inhaled foreign bodies, subglottic stenosis, retropharyngeal abscess, and angioedema. A frontal x-ray of the neck is not routinely performed, but it may demonstrate a steeple sign characteristic of croup due to narrowing of the trachea, which resembles a church steeple.  Blood tests and viral cultures are not routinely recommended, as they may cause increased agitation, potentially leading to further airway edema and obstruction. Viral cultures, obtained through nasopharyngeal aspiration, can confirm the etiology but are typically limited to research settings. Bacterial infection is considered a primary or secondary factor if the patient does not respond to standard treatments.[Clarke M, Allaire J. An evidence-based approach to the evaluation and treatment of croup in children. Pediatr Emerg Med Pract 2012; 9:1] Patients with recurrent croup need evaluation by a multidisciplinary team, including an otolaryngologist. A study by Quraishi et al in 2022 recommended some key points for evaluating recurrent croup. They concluded that children younger than 1 or 3 with a history of intubation presenting with recurrent croup should undergo triple endoscopy to identify structural airway abnormalities. If no airway abnormalities exist, patients should be screened for asthma and gastroesophageal reflux disease and treated empirically. The study concluded that the treatment of gastroesophageal reflux disease and asthma improves the symptoms of recurrent croup. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Stridor in Children -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Foreign body aspirations should be considered in cases where symptoms develop acutely, such as sudden coughing and choking in a previously healthy child. In the case of croup, beta-agonists should be avoided as they may potentially exacerbate upper airway obstruction. Antibiotics are necessary for bacterial tracheitis and epiglottitis. Both steroids and racemic epinephrine have proven to be effective for managing croup. Surgical drainage is necessary for retropharyngeal and peritonsillar abscesses. Severe laryngomalacia, laryngeal stenosis, critical tracheal stenosis, laryngeal and tracheal tumors, and foreign body aspiration require surgical correction.

retrieved_docs.title: Croup -- Treatment / Management -- Intubation
retrieved_docs.content: Approximately 0.2% of children require endotracheal intubation for respiratory support. Two retrospective studies found that fewer than 3% of children hospitalized for croup needed intubation. [15] Clinicians should use an endotracheal tube 0.5 size smaller than typical for the patient's age and size to account for airway narrowing due to swelling and inflammation.

retrieved_docs.title: Laryngotracheobronchitis -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Croup occurs more commonly in boys than in girls. It frequently occurs in children aged 6 months to 6 years old, with a peak incidence in children 12 months to 2 years of age. Most cases of croup occur from late autumn to early spring. Recurrent croup is rare unless there is a family history of the illness. Only a small percentage of patients require hospital admission. A population study conducted from 1999 to 2005 found that only 5.6% of children with croup required admission. Most patients can be safely discharged from the emergency department after appropriate management as discussed below.

retrieved_docs.title: Croup -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Croup is a common respiratory illness in children younger than 6, with peak incidence between 6 months and 3 years. This infection is most common in the fall and early winter but can occur year-round. Although typically mild and self-limiting, croup occasionally causes significant upper airway obstruction and respiratory distress. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Croup -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: The majority of patients with croup, more than 85%, exhibit mild symptoms, and severe croup is rare, fewer than 1%. In the United States, croup accounts annually for approximately 7% of pediatric hospitalizations, with fewer than 3% of admitted cases requiring intubation, and contributes to up to 15% of emergency department visits among children younger than 5. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Croup -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with croup are often assessed by primary care clinicians or in emergency departments. Educating healthcare providers is essential to ensure they understand that croup is generally a self-limiting condition, with most patients achieving full recovery through supportive care alone and having an excellent prognosis. Many may benefit from pharmacological therapy in the form of a single dose of dexamethasone. A community-based study of children with croup found no difference in symptom scores between 3 daily doses of prednisolone (2 mg/kg) and 1 dose of dexamethasone (0.6 mg/kg). [29] In another study on pediatric patients with croup, steroids effectively reduced croup symptoms, shortened hospital admission stays, and reduced the need for follow-up visits. [30]

retrieved_docs.title: Croup -- Treatment / Management -- Steroids
retrieved_docs.content: Oral dexamethasone is the preferred treatment for children with croup because it is effective, widely available, safe, and easily administered. A single dose is sufficient for most children. If symptoms persist for 3 to 4 days after the first dose of dexamethasone, a second dose can be administered.

retrieved_docs.title: Croup -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Spasmodic croup mostly occurs at night, with an abrupt onset and a short duration. Episodes may recur within the same night and persist for the next 2 to 3 nights. Spasmodic croup is sometimes called allergic croup, as it may be associated with a positive family history of allergies and atopy. The episodic symptoms of spasmodic croup in an otherwise healthy child differentiate it from viral croup. Symptoms are continuous, not intermittent, in viral croup. The typically benign yet dramatic presentation of spasmodic croup typically resolves with supportive care, including comfort measures and humidified air. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Croup -- Treatment / Management -- Steroids
retrieved_docs.content: The oral route is less invasive than an injection, making it more comfortable for children already experiencing respiratory distress. However, in cases where the child is unable to swallow, is vomiting, or has severe respiratory distress, intramuscular or intravenous dexamethasone may be necessary. Oral medication is also less costly and may be prescribed for home use in cases of mild croup.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2367
retrieved_docs.content: The term “nos” is classified as a child of “other upper respiratory disease,” and “other upper respiratory disease” in turn is a child of the broader category “اضطرابات أنفية، غير مصنف في موضع آخر” (nasal disorders, not classified elsewhere). The clinical procedure “control of epistaxis” is performed directly at the site identified as “nos” and its primary focus is “الرُعاف” (epistaxis). Additionally, “الرُعاف” is recognized as a child condition within the overarching group “اضطرابات أنفية، غير مصنف في موضع آخر.” This hierarchy links the nonspecific upper respiratory condition (“nos”) to a specific nasal disorder category and highlights that the management of epistaxis targets the “nos” site while addressing the condition “الرُعاف,” which itself belongs to the same broader nasal disorder classification.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 1 -- Community 166
retrieved_docs.content: a repository for miscellaneous respiratory interventions. Collectively, these entities form a network of diagnostic and therapeutic actions centered on the larynx, trachea, bronchi, and lung within the thoracic cavity, organized through parent‑child hierarchies, broader procedural groupings, and anatomical associations that are essential for coordinated patient care in respiratory and thoracic medicine.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1313
retrieved_docs.content: The larynx serves as an indirect site for various other non‑or therapeutic procedures on the respiratory system. Within this broader category, the specific intervention described as تركيب مسلك هوائي فموي بلعومي (placement of an oral‑pharyngeal airway) is classified as a child procedure. Recognizing that the larynx may be indirectly involved in such non‑therapeutic respiratory interventions, including the insertion of an oral‑pharyngeal airway, is important for clinicians when assessing airway management and potential procedural complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 1 -- Community 222
retrieved_docs.content: and parent‑child relationships organize diagnostic, therapeutic, and supportive interventions by anatomical site (larynx, trachea, bronchus, lung, mediastinum, thoracic cavity) and procedural type (direct vs. indirect, diagnostic vs. therapeutic), providing an integrated framework for understanding and managing respiratory and thoracic conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 857
retrieved_docs.content: The category “عملية في المجرى التنفسي” (procedure in the respiratory tract) serves as a parent classification for several specific interventions. Under this parent, “Diagnostic bronchoscopy and biopsy of bronchus” is identified as a distinct diagnostic procedure, as is “Other diagnostic procedures of respiratory tract and mediastinum,” which groups additional diagnostic techniques involving the airway and mediastinum. In addition to these diagnostic actions, the therapeutic set “الإجراءات العلاجية الخاصة بالسبيل التنفسي، غير مصنف في موضع آخر” (therapeutic procedures specific to the respiratory tract, not classified elsewhere) is also listed as a child of the same parent category. This hierarchical organization groups both diagnostic and therapeutic respiratory procedures under a common umbrella, facilitating clear classification, documentation, and management of airway‑related medical interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 4 -- Community 2936
retrieved_docs.content: الرُعاف (epistaxis) is classified as a child entity within اضطرابات أنفية، غير مصنف في موضع آخر (nasal disorders, not classified elsewhere), indicating that it is a specific type of nasal disorder that does not fit into other subcategories. Clinical attention to this condition centers on the control of epistaxis, which is identified as a primary focus of management for patients experiencing nasal bleeding. Recognizing الرُعاف as a distinct subset of unclassified nasal disorders facilitates targeted therapeutic strategies aimed at effectively controlling bleeding and improving patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1723
retrieved_docs.content: The term خديج designates infants who have completed 29–30 weeks of gestation, and this developmental stage is directly linked to the occurrence of متلازمة خلل نضج الرئة (pulmonary immaturity syndrome). Consequently, babies classified as خديج are at risk for respiratory complications arising from lung immaturity, highlighting the importance of recognizing this gestational age category when assessing and managing neonatal respiratory health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1907
retrieved_docs.content: The larynx serves as an indirect procedure site for other non‑or therapeutic procedures on the respiratory system. The specific intervention “Injection of larynx” includes as a subordinate step the procedure “تركيب مسلك هوائي فموي بلعومي” (placement of an oral‑pharyngeal airway). Likewise, the broader category “other non‑or therapeutic procedures on respiratory system” also lists “تركيب مسلك هوائي فموي بلعومي” as a child procedure. This demonstrates that placement of an oral‑pharyngeal airway is a common subprocedure both within targeted laryngeal injections and within general non‑therapeutic respiratory interventions, underscoring its relevance to airway management across multiple procedural contexts.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 2381
retrieved_docs.content: Injection of larynx is classified as a throat surgery and encompasses the specific procedure تركيب مسلك هوائي فموي بلعومي, which is listed as a child of Injection of larynx. In addition, Injection of locally-acting therapeutic substance into trachea is also categorized as a throat surgery. Both interventions involve therapeutic injections within the upper airway and represent distinct subtypes of throat surgery, illustrating the variety of surgical options available for managing laryngeal and tracheal conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 2146
retrieved_docs.content: The larynx serves as an indirect procedure site for other non‑or therapeutic procedures on the respiratory system, indicating that certain respiratory interventions that are not directly therapeutic to the larynx can nonetheless involve the larynx indirectly. This relationship highlights the importance of recognizing the larynx’s involvement when planning and performing non‑therapeutic respiratory procedures, as indirect effects on the larynx may impact airway function and patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 1707
retrieved_docs.content: Neonatal disorder (اضطراب لدى حديث الولادة) is a broad category of conditions affecting newborns. Respiratory distress syndrome is identified as a specific type of neonatal disorder, and the category also includes the child conditions fetal erythroblastosis (كثرة الأرومات الحمر الجنينية) and premature rupture of membranes (رضح الولادة). Prevention and control measures are applicable to the overall neonatal disorder as well as to each of its constituent conditions—respiratory distress syndrome, fetal erythroblastosis, and premature rupture of membranes—underscoring the importance of proactive management in newborn care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 235
retrieved_docs.content: أنفية، غير مصنف في موضع آخر also has الرُعاف as a child concept, reinforcing the connection between unspecified nasal disorders and bleeding from the nose. Overall, these relationships illustrate a hierarchical and functional network linking nasal‑septum disorders, their classification within upper‑respiratory disease taxonomy, and the corresponding diagnostic and therapeutic procedures—particularly those aimed at controlling epistaxis—thereby providing a comprehensive framework for understanding and managing nasal‑related conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 303
retrieved_docs.content: The category “عملية في المجرى التنفسي” (procedure in the respiratory tract) serves as a parent classification for several specific interventions. Under this parent, “Diagnostic bronchoscopy and biopsy of bronchus” is identified as a diagnostic procedure, as is “Excision of lung and bronchus,” which involves surgical removal of lung and bronchial tissue. “Other diagnostic procedures of respiratory tract and mediastinum” also falls within this same parent category, encompassing additional diagnostic techniques applied to the airway and mediastinal structures. In addition to these diagnostic actions, the child entry “الإجراءات العلاجية الخاصة بالسبيل التنفسي، غير مصنف في موضع آخر” (therapeutic procedures of the respiratory tract, not classified elsewhere) is linked to the same parent, representing therapeutic interventions that do not fit into other specific subcategories. This hierarchical organization groups both diagnostic and therapeutic respiratory procedures under a common parent, facilitating clear categorization and systematic management of airway‑related clinical interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 1981
retrieved_docs.content: Procedures on Nose are medical interventions that address nasal disorders (اِضطِراب أنفي). Within this category, control of epistaxis is identified as a specific type of Procedure on Nose, indicating that managing nosebleeds is a subset of nasal procedural care. This relationship highlights that interventions aimed at controlling epistaxis are directly linked to the broader management of nasal disorders, emphasizing the role of targeted procedural treatment in the evaluation and care of patients with nasal pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 1 -- Community 166
retrieved_docs.content: tracheostomy is placed under other diagnostic procedures of the respiratory tract and mediastinum, a child of عملية في المجرى التنفسي, and is reciprocally related to throat surgery, illustrating the diagnostic‑surgical interplay. The trachea serves as a direct procedure site for tracheoscopy and as an indirect site for both temporary and permanent tracheostomy, underscoring its role in airway interventions. Injection of a locally‑acting therapeutic substance into the trachea is also categorized as throat surgery, further overlapping laryngeal and tracheal therapeutic procedures. Finally, therapeutic procedures of the respiratory tract not classified elsewhere (Arabic الإجراءات العلاجية الخاصة بالسبيل التنفسي، غير مصنف في موضع آخر) is a catch‑all child of عملية في المجرى التنفسي, providing a repository for miscellaneous respiratory interventions. Diagnostic procedures on the larynx and trachea are organized under the “other diagnostic procedures of respiratory tract and mediastinum” subcategory. Injection of the larynx is identified as a type of throat surgery and has a child procedure—placement of an oral‑pharyngeal airway (تركيب مسلك هوائي فموي بلعومي). Injection of a locally‑acting therapeutic substance into the trachea is likewise classified as throat surgery. The placement of an oral‑pharyngeal airway further has a child procedure described as “other non‑or therapeutic procedures on respiratory system,” reflecting a more specific therapeutic intervention within the airway management hierarchy.
